items.prompt_md: In the sorted-array two-pointer sum, if `numbers[lo] + numbers[hi]` is **smaller** than the target, what should happen next?
options: Move `hi` left, since we need a smaller sum
Move `lo` right, since we need a bigger sum
Restart the scan from the smallest unused pair
items.answer: Move `lo` right, since we need a bigger sum
items.explanation_md: `hi` already points at the largest remaining value, so moving it left only shrinks the sum further. Only advancing `lo` to a bigger value can raise the sum toward the target.

items.prompt_md: The array is sorted and you must find whether two elements sum exactly to a target, returning their positions, using O(1) extra space. Which approach fits?
options: Keep a hash map of values seen so far and look up each complement
Start pointers at both ends and move whichever one corrects the sum toward the target
Binary search for `target - x` for every element `x`
items.answer: Start pointers at both ends and move whichever one corrects the sum toward the target
items.explanation_md: Sorted + pair-sum + O(1) space is the fingerprint for the sorted-array two-pointer scan. A hash map would work but costs O(n) space that sortedness makes unnecessary.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the two-pointer scan over an already-sorted array of length n (ignoring any sort step)?
items.explanation_md: Each pointer moves inward at most n times total across the whole scan and never backtracks, so the pass is O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
